$meta.description: Seed regulation categories converted from hardcoded berkeley_regulations.py

zoning_codes.title: Berkeley Zoning Code Definitions
zoning_codes.summary: Berkeley has 30+ zoning districts organized into Residential (R-), Commercial (C-), Mixed Use (MU-/MULI), Environmental Safety (ES-R), and Manufacturing (M) categories. The 'H' suffix denotes the Hillside Overlay Zone with stricter height, fire safety, and view protection rules.
zoning_codes.details:
Berkeley's zoning code (Title 23 of the Municipal Code) was comprehensively updated in December 2021 — the first major revision since 1999.

ZONE CODE SUFFIXES:
- 'H' = Hillside Overlay — applies additional height restrictions, fire safety requirements, vegetation management, and view protection rules. Hillside zones are generally NOT eligible for Middle Housing due to fire hazard concerns.
- 'A' in R-2A/R-2AH = higher density variant (1 unit per 1,650 sqft vs 2,500 sqft in R-2).

Use lookup_regulation with a specific zone_code (e.g., 'R-1H') for details on individual zones.

hillside_overlay.title: Hillside Overlay Zone (H Suffix)
hillside_overlay.summary: The 'H' suffix in Berkeley zoning codes designates the Hillside Overlay Zone — a regulatory overlay that adds restrictions on top of the base zoning district for fire safety, slope stability, and neighborhood character.
hillside_overlay.details:
WHAT THE H SUFFIX MEANS:
The 'H' in zones like R-1H, R-2H, R-2AH, R-3H, and R-4H stands for Hillside Overlay Zone (BMC 23.210.020). It is NOT a height designation — it is a geographic overlay applied to properties in Berkeley's hillside areas.

KEY RESTRICTIONS:
- Stricter height limits than flatland equivalents
- Fire safety requirements (vegetation management, fire-resistant materials, emergency access)
- View protection rules — new construction must not unreasonably obstruct views from neighboring properties
- Slope-based development standards — steeper slopes face more restrictions
- When overlay standards conflict with the base district, the overlay rules prevail

IMPACT ON DEVELOPMENT:
- Middle Housing Ordinance does NOT apply to hillside zones
- ADUs are permitted but limited to 16 ft height (1 story)
- SB 9 lot splitting is eligible in R-1H
- ES-R (Environmental Safety — Residential) is the most restrictive hillside zone: only 20% lot coverage, ADUs limited to 500 sqft

GEOGRAPHIC CONTEXT:
Hillside zones primarily cover the Berkeley Hills east of the Hayward Fault, including areas affected by the 1923 fire and within active fire hazard severity zones.

adu_rules.title: Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU) & JADU Regulations
adu_rules.summary: Berkeley permits ADUs up to 850 sqft (1BR) or 1,000 sqft (2BR), plus Junior ADUs up to 500 sqft within the existing home. One story max, 16 ft height. Ministerial approval (no public hearing).
adu_rules.details:
SIZE LIMITS:
- ADU (1 bedroom): up to 850 sqft
- ADU (2 bedrooms): up to 1,000 sqft
- Junior ADU (JADU): up to 500 sqft, must be entirely within the existing or proposed single-family residence
- In ES-R zones: ADU limited to 500 sqft

HEIGHT & STORIES:
- Maximum 1 story
- 16 ft height limit (all zones including hillside)
- Detached ADU: must comply with lot coverage limits

APPROVAL PROCESS:
- Ministerial review — no public hearing or discretionary approval
- Building permit required
- State law (CA Gov Code 65852.2) preempts most local barriers

RESTRICTIONS:
- Short-term rental prohibited (deed restriction)
- Cannot be sold separately from primary residence (except condos under AB 1033)
- Parking: max 1 space per ADU bedroom; no parking required if within 1/2 mile of transit

ELIGIBILITY:
- Allowed in all residential zones (R-1 through R-4, MU-R, ES-R)
- One ADU + one JADU per single-family lot
- Multi-family properties: up to 2 detached ADUs + conversion of non-livable space (laundry rooms, storage, etc.)

sb9_lot_splitting.title: SB 9 Urban Lot Splitting
sb9_lot_splitting.summary: SB 9 allows splitting a single-family lot into two parcels and building a duplex on each, for up to 4 total units. Only eligible in R-1 and R-1H zones with a minimum 2,400 sqft lot.
sb9_lot_splitting.details:
ELIGIBLE ZONES:
- R-1 (Single Family Residential)
- R-1H (Single Family Residential — Hillside Overlay)
- NOT eligible in R-2, R-3, R-4, or commercial zones

LOT REQUIREMENTS:
- Minimum existing lot size: 2,400 sqft
- Each resulting lot must be at least 1,200 sqft
- No flag lots or lots without street frontage

WHAT YOU CAN BUILD:
- Up to 2 units per resulting lot (duplex)
- Maximum 4 total units (2 lots x 2 units each)
- 800 sqft minimum per unit

APPROVAL PROCESS:
- Ministerial approval — no public hearing
- No discretionary review
- Cannot be denied if all objective standards are met

RESTRICTIONS:
- Owner must occupy one unit for 3 years after split
- Cannot be used on properties withdrawn from rent control within past 15 years (Ellis Act)
- Cannot demolish rent-stabilized housing
- Not available for historic landmarks
- Units cannot be used for short-term rentals

middle_housing.title: Middle Housing Ordinance
middle_housing.summary: Effective November 1, 2025, Berkeley's Middle Housing Ordinance allows duplexes through 8-plexes in most residential zones. Projects with 2-6 units meeting objective standards get 30-day ministerial approval.
middle_housing.details:
EFFECTIVE DATE: November 1, 2025

ELIGIBLE ZONES AND UNIT CAPS:
- R-1: up to 5 units
- R-2: up to 6 units
- R-2A: up to 7 units
- MU-R/MUR: up to 7 units
- Maximum 3 stories and 8 units in any zone

NOT ELIGIBLE:
- Hillside Overlay zones (R-1H, R-2H, R-2AH, R-3H, R-4H)
- High fire hazard severity zones in Berkeley Hills
- R-3, R-4 zones (already allow multi-family by right)

APPROVAL PROCESS:
- 2-6 units meeting objective development standards: 30-day ministerial approval (no public hearing)
- 7-8 units or projects not meeting standards: standard discretionary review

LOT COVERAGE:
- Increased to 60% for Middle Housing projects (vs 45% standard)

AFFORDABILITY BONUS:
- Additional units allowed if affordable units are included

EXPECTED IMPACT:
- Estimated ~1,700 new units over 8 years (conservative)
- Applies to Downtown, Elmwood, Fourth St., North Shattuck, Solano Ave., Telegraph Ave., Lorin, West Berkeley, San Pablo Ave., University Ave.

beso.title: Building Emissions Saving Ordinance (BESO)
beso.summary: At the point of sale, Berkeley requires an energy assessment for buildings over 600 sqft. Sellers must obtain an energy audit; if upgrades are needed, a $5,000 escrow deposit may be required.
beso.details:
WHEN TRIGGERED:
- At point of sale for residential buildings over 600 sqft
- Applies to both single-family and multi-family properties

REQUIREMENTS:
- Seller must obtain a Home Energy Score or energy audit
- Energy audit cost: typically $300-$750
- If upgrades are recommended, a $5,000 escrow deposit may be required to ensure compliance

POTENTIAL UPGRADE COSTS:
- Minor (insulation, weatherstripping): $2,000-$5,000
- Moderate (water heater, windows): $5,000-$15,000
- Major (HVAC replacement, extensive insulation): $15,000-$25,000+

EXEMPTIONS:
- New construction (already meets current energy code)
- Recent major renovations with updated energy systems

BUYER IMPACT:
- Factor BESO costs into your offer price
- Request the energy audit report during due diligence
- Negotiate who pays for required upgrades

transfer_tax.title: City of Berkeley Transfer Tax
transfer_tax.summary: Berkeley charges a transfer tax on property sales: 1.5% for properties up to $1.8M and 2.5% for properties above $1.8M. A partial rebate is available for seismic retrofitting.
transfer_tax.details:
TAX RATES (as of Measure P, 2024):
- Up to $1,800,000: 1.5% of sale price
- Above $1,800,000: 2.5% of sale price

NOTE: The rate applies to the ENTIRE sale price based on which tier it falls into, not incrementally.

EXAMPLES:
- $1,200,000 sale: $18,000 tax (1.5%)
- $2,000,000 sale: $50,000 tax (2.5%)
- $3,000,000 sale: $75,000 tax (2.5%)

SEISMIC REBATE:
- Up to 1/3 of the city portion (~0.5% of sale price up to $1.8M tier) may be rebated for qualified seismic strengthening work
- Must complete seismic retrofit within specified timeframe

WHO PAYS:
- Traditionally split between buyer and seller, but negotiable
- Often the seller pays in Berkeley's market

COMPARISON:
- Alameda County also charges a separate transfer tax
- Berkeley's rates are among the highest in the East Bay

rent_control.title: Berkeley Rent Control & Tenant Protections
rent_control.summary: Berkeley has one of the strongest rent control laws in California, covering approximately 29,000 rental units. Vacancy decontrol has been in effect since 1999.
rent_control.details:
COVERAGE:
- Approximately 29,000 rental units covered
- Over 20,000 units have regulated rents
- Governed by the Rent Stabilization and Eviction for Good Cause Ordinance (BMC Ch. 13.76)

EXEMPT PROPERTIES:
- Units built after June 1980 (Costa-Hawkins)
- Single-family homes and condos (if separately owned)
- Owner-occupied duplexes and triplexes (owner in one unit)
- Government-subsidized housing
- Newly constructed ADUs (for 15 years per AB 1482)

VACANCY DECONTROL:
- Since January 1999 (Costa-Hawkins Act)
- Landlords can set rent at market rate for new tenancies
- Once tenant moves in, rent increases are regulated

RENT INCREASE LIMITS:
- Annual adjustment set by Rent Board (typically 2-5%)
- Based on Consumer Price Index (CPI)
- Capital improvement pass-throughs allowed with Board approval

JUST CAUSE EVICTION:
- Landlords must have legally specified cause to evict
- Includes non-payment, lease violation, owner move-in, Ellis Act withdrawal
- Relocation payments required for no-fault evictions

INVESTOR RELEVANCE:
- Rent control affects cash flow projections for investment properties
- Vacancy decontrol means turnover is financially significant
- New ADUs are exempt for 15 years — attractive for investors

permitting.title: Berkeley Permitting Process Overview
permitting.summary: Berkeley permits fall into two categories: ministerial (staff-level, no hearing) and discretionary (requires public hearing). ADUs and SB 9 projects get ministerial approval.
permitting.details:
PERMIT TYPES:
- Building Permit: required for all construction/renovation
- Use Permit (UP): for uses not permitted by right
- Administrative Use Permit (AUP): simplified discretionary review
- Zoning Certificate: confirms permitted use

MINISTERIAL (NO HEARING):
- ADUs and JADUs
- SB 9 lot splits and duplexes
- Middle Housing projects (2-6 units meeting objective standards)
- Interior renovations
- Like-for-like replacements

DISCRETIONARY (PUBLIC HEARING):
- New multi-family construction (7+ units)
- Commercial projects
- Variance requests
- Projects not meeting objective development standards

TYPICAL TIMELINES:
- ADU permit: 60 days (state-mandated)
- SB 9: 60 days
- Middle Housing (2-6 units): 30 days
- Standard building permit: 4-8 weeks for plan check
- Use Permit/discretionary: 3-6 months or longer

FEES:
- Vary by project scope and valuation
- Building permit fees based on construction valuation
- Plan check fees (typically 65-80% of permit fee)
- Traffic engineering review: minimum $90
- School impact fees, park fees may apply for new units
